modification:
Diagnosis: The agent is defaulting to exactly 50% in ranging/neutral conditions and ignoring the empirical base rate that BTC 5-min candles close UP slightly more than 50% of the time (the market price itself often reflects this at ~50.5%). By anchoring to 50% instead of the market price as the baseline, the agent adds no value and achieves poor Brier scores when the outcome is YES.
Old text: - If no momentum or news, default to 50% with low confidence
New text: - If no momentum or news, default to the current market price as your baseline estimate with low confidence
Expected effect: By using the market price as the neutral baseline instead of a hard 50%, the agent will better capture any small structural bias already priced in, reducing Brier score in no-edge scenarios.

Diagnosis: When the market price is near 50% and there's no momentum, the agent defaults to exactly 50% instead of deferring to the market price. In several worst cases, the market price was 0.495 or 0.505, but the agent rounded to 0.5, ignoring the small signal embedded in the market price itself.
Old text: - If no momentum or news, default to the current market price as your baseline estimate with low confidence
New text: - If no momentum or news, output the current market price exactly as your estimate (do not round to 0.5) with low confidence
Expected effect: The agent will preserve the market's own probability signal rather than discarding small deviations from 50%, reducing unnecessary error when the market has already priced in a slight directional lean.

Diagnosis: The agent is ignoring the market price as a signal, defaulting to 50% even when the market is pricing meaningfully away from 50% (e.g., 61.5%). The market price itself contains aggregated information the agent should incorporate rather than override with a flat base rate.
Old text: - Never deviate more than 10pp from 50% on statistical patterns alone — edge is thin at this timescale
New text: - Never deviate more than 10pp from 50% on statistical patterns alone — edge is thin at this timescale
- Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 40% own analysis and 60% market price, then report the blended result
Expected effect: By partially deferring to the market price when it diverges significantly from 50%, the agent will avoid large negative edges like the -0.115 case and produce estimates closer to the true probability.

Diagnosis: The agent ignores the market price when it deviates less than 5pp from 50%, even when the market price contains meaningful signal (e.g., 0.615 is a strong directional signal that was completely disregarded). The 5pp threshold is too high, causing the agent to systematically discard actionable market information.
Old text: - Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 40% own analysis and 60% market price, then report the blended result
New text: - Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 2pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 40% own analysis and 60% market price, then report the blended result
Expected effect: By lowering the threshold from 5pp to 2pp, the agent will incorporate market pricing signal more often, reducing cases where a clearly directional market price (e.g., 0.615) is ignored in favor of a flat 50% estimate.

Diagnosis: The agent over-weights the market price signal when it deviates even slightly from 50%, blending 60% market price into its estimate even when the market price is only 2pp away from 50% (e.g., 0.495 or 0.505). This causes the agent to anchor too heavily on potentially noisy market prices rather than maintaining calibrated independence, and in the worst case (32% prediction, actual YES) the market price dragged the estimate far from the base rate without sufficient justification.
Old text: - Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 2pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 40% own analysis and 60% market price, then report the blended result
New text: - Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 60% own analysis and 40% market price, then report the blended result. Deviations smaller than 5pp from 50% should be treated as noise and ignored.
Expected effect: By raising the threshold for market price incorporation and reducing its weight, the agent will stay closer to the base rate when market signals are weak, avoiding large errors caused by over-trusting small or noisy market price deviations.

Diagnosis: The agent under-weights the market price when it deviates significantly from 50%, using only a 40% weight on the market signal even when the market is highly informed (e.g., 78.5% or 25.5%). This causes the agent to anchor too strongly on its own weak statistical signals and produce estimates that diverge badly from an informative market consensus.
Old text: Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 60% own analysis and 40% market price, then report the blended result. Deviations smaller than 5pp from 50% should be treated as noise and ignored.
New text: Treat the market price as an informative prior: if market price deviates more than 5pp from 50%, incorporate it by weighting your estimate 30% own analysis and 70% market price, then report the blended result. If market price deviates more than 20pp from 50%, weight your estimate 10% own analysis and 90% market price, as the market is likely incorporating real information you cannot see. Deviations smaller than 5pp from 50% should be treated as noise and ignored.
Expected effect: By deferring more strongly to large market price deviations, the agent will avoid catastrophic mispredictions when the market has strong directional conviction that the agent's weak statistical signals cannot explain.
